answers_clean: Abdominal pain refers to any discomfort or unpleasant sensation felt in the abdomen region of the body. It can be caused by various factors such as gastrointestinal issues, appendicitis, kidney stones, urinary tract infection, pancreatitis, endometriosis, irritable bowel syndrome, food allergies, muscle strains, menstrual cramps, pregnancy complications, and many other conditions . The severity of abdominal pain may range from mild to severe and constant. In some cases, it may also lead to nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, fever, bloating, gas, constipation, and weight loss .

Yes, I do have some information about acupuncture. It is an ancient form of Chinese medicine that involves inserting thin needles into specific points on the body to stimulate the flow of energy or chi along meridians. This practice has been used for thousands of years in China as well as other Asian countries such as Japan, Korea, Vietnam, etc., and it is now becoming more popular worldwide due to its effectiveness in treating various health conditions including pain management, stress relief, insomnia, depression, anxiety, infertility, digestive issues, etc.

An adhesion is an abnormal band of fibrous tissue that connects two tissues together in the body. They can occur anywhere in the body but commonly develop within the peritoneal cavity after surgery or injury to organs such as the bowel.

Yes, I do have some general information about health disparities in the United States. According to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), Black or African Americans experience higher rates of chronic diseases such as heart disease, stroke, diabetes, obesity, asthma, cancer, and kidney disease compared with White people . These differences may be due to social determinants of health, including access to quality education, employment opportunities, safe housing, healthy food options, and healthcare services .

An allergy is an adverse immune response to a substance or agent that is generally harmless in most people. It occurs when the body's immune system overreacts to a specific protein found in a particular food, drug, insect venom, pollen, pet dander, or other substances. The symptoms of allergies can range from mild to severe and may include sneezing, itchy eyes, runny nose, hives, swelling, asthma attacks, vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal pain, eczema, rashes, wheezing, shortness of breath, chest tightness, hypotension, syncope, and even death.

Amyloidosis refers to a group of rare diseases in which abnormal deposits of amyloid protein accumulate in various tissues or organs throughout the body . These proteins can interfere with normal organ function and lead to serious health complications if left untreated. There are several types of amyloidosis that differ in their underlying cause and clinical presentation. The most common form is secondary amyloidosis, which develops as a complication of another disease such as chronic inflammatory conditions, lymphomas, multiple myeloma, or autoimmune disorders. Primary amyloidosis occurs when an unknown mutation causes misfolded light chains to be produced by plasma cells in the bone marrow.

Anaphylaxis is an acute allergic reaction that can affect multiple organ systems in the body, including the skin, respiratory tract, gastrointestinal tract, cardiovascular system, and nervous system . It typically occurs within minutes to hours after exposure to an allergen, such as food or medication . The symptoms of anaphylaxis may include hives, swelling, pruritus, angioedema, wheezing, dyspnea, hypotension, syncope, and loss of consciousness .

Angina refers to chest pain or discomfort that occurs due to reduced blood flow to the heart muscle. It can be caused by coronary artery disease (CAD), which involves narrowing of the arteries that supply blood to the heart. The most common symptom of CAD is angina, which typically presents as a constrictive sensation in the center of the chest that may feel like pressure, heaviness, tightness, fullness, or pain.

Ankylosing spondylitis (AS) is an inflammatory rheumatic disease that primarily affects the axial skeleton, including the sacroiliac joints, vertebrae, and paraspinal ligaments . It can also affect other parts of the body such as the eyes, intestines, and skin. The exact cause of AS is unknown but it appears to be hereditary in some cases. Genetic factors may play a role in determining who develops this condition.

Yes, I do! Antioxidants are molecules that can neutralize free radicals in the body by donating an electron to them. Free radicals are unstable atoms or molecules with one or more unpaired electrons that can cause damage to cells and tissues if not neutralized. Antioxidants play an important role in preventing oxidative stress, which is linked to many chronic diseases such as cancer, heart disease, diabetes, and Alzheimer's disease. There are several types of antioxidants found naturally in foods such as vitamins A, C, E, beta-carotene, lycopene, coenzyme Q10 (CoQ10), glutathione, and polyphenols. Supplemental forms of these antioxidants may be taken in capsule form for their health benefits. However, it is important to note that high doses of some antioxidant supplements may actually increase the risk of certain diseases, so it is always best to consult with a healthcare provider before taking any new supplements.
answers_noisy: If you have abdominal pain, it could be caused by several factors such as gastritis, food poisoning, gastroenteritis, appendicitis, pancreatitis, diverticulitis, irritable bowel syndrome (IBS), Crohn's disease, ulcerative colitis, kidney stones, urinary tract infection (UTI), ectopic pregnancy, ovarian cysts, endometriosis, or cancer. It would be best to consult with a healthcare professional for proper diagnosis and treatment.

Acupuncture has been shown to be effective in treating various types of chronic pain, including back pain, neck pain, arthritis pain, headaches, and migraines. However, more research is needed to fully understand how acupuncture works and whether it is an effective treatment option for different types of pain.

The term “stuff” can refer to different types of materials or tissues within the body. If these materials stick together due to an injury or disease process, this may be referred to as adhesions. Adhesions occur naturally in certain parts of the body such as the peritoneum (the lining of the abdominal cavity). However, excessive adhesion formation can cause problems by interfering with normal organ function.

Yes, there is information available about specific health concerns among African Americans. According to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), African Americans have higher rates of some chronic diseases such as heart disease, stroke, type 2 diabetes, and certain cancers compared to other racial groups in the United States . This may be due to various factors including genetics, environmental exposures, socioeconomic status, access to healthcare, and cultural beliefs related to dietary habits and lifestyle choices. It is important for individuals of all races and ethnicities to follow healthy lifestyles, which include getting enough physical activity, eating a balanced diet, maintaining a healthy weight, not smoking tobacco products, limiting alcohol consumption, and managing stress levels. Additionally, it is recommended that individuals get regular check-ups with their primary care provider to screen for these conditions early and take steps towards prevention and management if necessary.

That's an interesting question! It sounds like you might be referring to allergic or hypersensitivity reactions. These can occur in response to various substances such as food, medications, insect venom, latex, pollen, dust mites, pet dander, or other environmental triggers. When these types of reactions involve the immune system, they are called "allergies" .

The condition you may be referring to could be obstructive jaundice or cholestatic liver disease. This occurs when there is an obstruction in the bile ducts inside or outside of the liver which prevents the flow of bile from the liver to the intestines. As a result, bile can build up in the liver and cause damage. Symptoms include yellowing of the skin (jaundice), dark urine, light-colored stool, fatigue, itching, abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, loss of appetite, weight loss, and clay-colored stools. It's important for patients with symptoms like these to see their doctor right away as this condition can lead to serious complications if left untreated.

This condition is known as anaphylaxis. It can be caused by exposure to various triggers such as food, insect stings, medications, latex, pollen, or other environmental factors. Anaphylactic reactions typically occur within minutes to hours of exposure and may involve multiple organ systems including the skin, respiratory tract, gastrointestinal system, cardiovascular system, and nervous system. Symptoms of anaphylaxis include difficulty breathing, hives, flushing, abdominal pain, diarrhea, dizziness, headache, and loss of consciousness. If you experience any symptoms suggestive of anaphylaxis, seek immediate medical attention.

Angina refers to a type of chest pain that typically occurs with physical exertion and is caused by reduced oxygen supply to the heart muscle due to narrowed coronary arteries. It can be described as a squeezing, pressure, tightness, fullness, or pain in the center of the chest that may spread to the neck, jaw, arms, shoulder, back, or abdomen .

The condition you are referring to is known as degenerative disc disease (DDD). It can occur in any part of the spine but most commonly affects the lumbar region. DDD involves changes in the intervertebral discs such as herniation, bulging, protrusion, and narrowing of the disc space. These changes may lead to compression of nerve roots resulting in pain radiating down the legs. Other symptoms include weakness, numbness, tingling sensations, and muscle spasms. Diagnosis is typically made through physical examination and imaging tests such as X-rays, CT scans, and MRI. Treatment options vary depending on the severity of symptoms and may involve medication, physical therapy, surgery, or lifestyle modifications.

Angina refers to chest pain caused by reduced blood flow to the heart muscle due to coronary artery disease (CAD). It can be classified as stable angina or unstable angina. Stable angina occurs during physical exertion and resolves with rest. Unstable angina may occur at rest and often worsens over time. While both types of angina are associated with CAD, they differ in terms of their pathophysiology, prognosis, and management strategies.

reference_answers: Your abdomen extends from below your chest to your groin. Some people call it the stomach, but your abdomen contains many other important organs. Pain in the abdomen can come from any one of them. The pain may start somewhere else, such as your chest. Severe pain doesn't always mean a serious problem. Nor does mild pain mean a problem is not serious. Call your healthcare provider if mild pain lasts a week or more or if you have pain with other symptoms. Get medical help immediately if - You have abdominal pain that is sudden and sharp - You also have pain in your chest, neck or shoulder - You're vomiting blood or have blood in your stool - Your abdomen is stiff, hard and tender to touch - You can't move your bowels, especially if you're also vomiting

Summary : Acupuncture has been practiced in China and other Asian countries for thousands of years. Acupuncture involves stimulating specific points on the body. This is most often done by inserting thin needles through the skin, to cause a change in the physical functions of the body. Research has shown that acupuncture reduces nausea and vomiting after surgery and chemotherapy. It can also relieve pain. Researchers don't fully understand how acupuncture works. It might aid the activity of your body's pain-killing chemicals. It also might affect how you release chemicals that regulate blood pressure and flow. NIH: National Center for Complementary and Integrative Health

Adhesions are bands of scar-like tissue. Normally, internal tissues and organs have slippery surfaces so they can shift easily as the body moves. Adhesions cause tissues and organs to stick together. They might connect the loops of the intestines to each other, to nearby organs, or to the wall of the abdomen. They can pull sections of the intestines out of place. This may block food from passing through the intestine. Adhesions can occur anywhere in the body. But they often form after surgery on the abdomen. Almost everyone who has surgery on the abdomen gets adhesions. Some adhesions don't cause any problems. But when they partly or completely block the intestines, they cause symptoms such as - Severe abdominal pain or cramping - Vomiting - Bloating - An inability to pass gas - Constipation Adhesions can sometimes cause infertility in women by preventing fertilized eggs from reaching the uterus. No tests are available to detect adhesions. Doctors usually find them during surgery to diagnose other problems. Some adhesions go away by themselves. If they partly block your intestines, a diet low in fiber can allow food to move easily through the affected area. If you have a complete intestinal obstruction, it is life threatening. You should get immediate medical attention and may need surgery. NIH: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases

Summary : Every racial or ethnic group has specific health concerns. Differences in the health of groups can result from - Genetics - Environmental factors - Access to care - Cultural factors On this page, you'll find links to health issues that affect African Americans.

An allergy is a reaction by your immune system to something that does not bother most other people. People who have allergies often are sensitive to more than one thing. Substances that often cause reactions are - Pollen - Dust mites - Mold spores - Pet dander - Food - Insect stings - Medicines Normally, your immune system fights germs. It is your body's defense system. In most allergic reactions, however, it is responding to a false alarm. Genes and the environment probably both play a role. Allergies can cause a variety of symptoms such as a runny nose, sneezing, itching, rashes, swelling, or asthma. Allergies can range from minor to severe. Anaphylaxis is a severe reaction that can be life-threatening. Doctors use skin and blood tests to diagnose allergies. Treatments include medicines, allergy shots, and avoiding the substances that cause the reactions. NIH: National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases

Amyloidosis occurs when abnormal proteins called amyloids build up and form deposits. The deposits can collect in organs such as the kidney and heart. This can cause the organs to become stiff and unable to work the way they should. There are three main types of amyloidosis: - Primary - with no known cause - Secondary - caused by another disease, including some types of cancer - Familial - passed down through genes Symptoms can vary, depending upon which organs are affected. Treatment depends on the type of amyloidosis you have. The goal is to help with symptoms and limit the production of proteins. If another disease is the cause, it needs to be treated.

Anaphylaxis is a serious allergic reaction. It can begin very quickly, and symptoms may be life-threatening. The most common causes are reactions to foods (especially peanuts), medications, and stinging insects. Other causes include exercise and exposure to latex. Sometimes no cause can be found. It can affect many organs: - Skin - itching, hives, redness, swelling - Nose - sneezing, stuffy nose, runny nose - Mouth - itching, swelling of the lips or tongue - Throat - itching, tightness, trouble swallowing, swelling of the back of the throat - Chest - shortness of breath, coughing, wheezing, chest pain or tightness - Heart - weak pulse, passing out, shock - Gastrointestinal tract - vomiting, diarrhea, cramps - Nervous system - dizziness or fainting If someone is having a serious allergic reaction, call 9-1-1. If an auto-injector is available, give the person the injection right away. NIH: National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases

Angina is chest pain or discomfort you feel when there is not enough blood flow to your heart muscle. Your heart muscle needs the oxygen that the blood carries. Angina may feel like pressure or a squeezing pain in your chest. It may feel like indigestion. You may also feel pain in your shoulders, arms, neck, jaw, or back. Angina is a symptom of coronary artery disease (CAD), the most common heart disease. CAD happens when a sticky substance called plaque builds up in the arteries that supply blood to the heart, reducing blood flow. There are three types of angina: - Stable angina is the most common type. It happens when the heart is working harder than usual. Stable angina has a regular pattern. Rest and medicines usually help. - Unstable angina is the most dangerous. It does not follow a pattern and can happen without physical exertion. It does not go away with rest or medicine. It is a sign that you could have a heart attack soon. - Variant angina is rare. It happens when you are resting. Medicines can help. Not all chest pain or discomfort is angina. If you have chest pain, you should see your health care provider. NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute

Ankylosing spondylitis is a type of arthritis of the spine. It causes inflammation between your vertebrae, which are the bones that make up your spine, and in the joints between your spine and pelvis. In some people, it can affect other joints. AS is more common and more severe in men. It often runs in families. The cause is unknown, but it is likely that both genes and factors in the environment play a role. Early symptoms of AS include back pain and stiffness. These problems often start in late adolescence or early adulthood. Over time, AS can fuse your vertebrae together, limiting movement. Some people have symptoms that come and go. Others have severe, ongoing pain. A diagnosis of AS is based on your medical history and a physical examination. You may also have imaging or blood tests. AS has no cure, but medicines can relieve symptoms and may keep the disease from getting worse. Eating a healthy diet, not smoking, and exercising can also help. In rare cases, you may need surgery to straighten the spine. NIH: National Institute of Arthritis and Musculoskeletal and Skin Disease

Summary : Antioxidants are man-made or natural substances that may prevent or delay some types of cell damage. Antioxidants are found in many foods, including fruits and vegetables. They are also available as dietary supplements. Examples of antioxidants include - Beta-carotene - Lutein - Lycopene - Selenium - Vitamin A - Vitamin C - Vitamin E Vegetables and fruits are rich sources of antioxidants. There is good evidence that eating a diet with lots of vegetables and fruits is healthy and lowers risks of certain diseases. But it isn't clear whether this is because of the antioxidants, something else in the foods, or other factors. High-dose supplements of antioxidants may be linked to health risks in some cases. For example, high doses of beta-carotene may increase the risk of lung cancer in smokers. High doses of vitamin E may increase risks of prostate cancer and one type of stroke. Antioxidant supplements may also interact with some medicines. To minimize risk, tell you of your health care providers about any antioxidants you use. NIH: National Center for Complementary and Integrative Health

answers_clean: An abscess is an infection that forms in tissues or organs within the body, usually due to bacteria. It can occur anywhere in the body but most commonly develops in the skin, lungs, teeth, lymph nodes, appendix, or other areas of the gastrointestinal tract. The pus-filled cavity formed by the infection may be surrounded by inflamed tissue called granulation tissue.

Acute lymphocytic leukemia (ALL) is a type of cancer that affects white blood cells called lymphocytes or T-cells. It occurs when there is an abnormal proliferation of immature B-lymphoid precursors in the bone marrow. The disease can be classified into two types based on whether it involves both myeloid and lymphoid lineages (biphenotypic ALL), or only one lineage (monophenotypic ALL).

The adrenal glands are small organs located above the kidneys that produce hormones such as cortisol, aldosterone, and sex steroids . Adrenocortical carcinoma (ACC), which originates from the outer layer of the adrenal gland, accounts for less than 1% of all cancers worldwide . ACC is a rare malignant tumor with an annual incidence rate of approximately 0.7-2 cases per million people in Western countries , but it has been reported to be higher among Hispanic populations .

Yes, I do! Air pollution is defined as the presence of harmful substances in the air that can negatively impact human health. These pollutants include particulate matter (PM), ozone, nitrogen dioxide (NO2), sulfur dioxide (SO2), carbon monoxide (CO), lead, and volatile organic compounds (VOCs). Exposure to these pollutants has been linked to respiratory diseases such as asthma, lung cancer, heart disease, stroke, and other chronic conditions .

An Alzheimer’s caregiver refers to an individual who provides unpaid assistance or support to a person with Alzheimer’s disease or other forms of dementia in their daily activities such as bathing, eating, dressing, etc. The role can be demanding both physically and emotionally due to the progressive nature of the condition.

Yes, I do have information about anabolic steroids. They are synthetic derivatives of testosterone that are used to increase muscle mass and improve athletic performance. However, long-term use can lead to serious side effects such as heart disease, liver damage, infertility, and aggressive behavior. Anemia refers to a condition in which there is a decrease in the number of red blood cells or hemoglobin concentration in the circulating blood stream . It can be caused by various factors such as nutritional deficiencies, chronic diseases, genetic disorders, medications, chemotherapy, and other treatments.

It can be inflicted by various animals such as dogs, cats, rodents, insects, reptiles, birds, and marine life. The severity of an animal bite depends on several factors, including the type of animal involved, its size, the force with which it bites, and whether there was pre-existing disease in the animal's mouth .

Yes, I do! Antibiotics are drugs that kill or inhibit the growth of bacteria. They can also be used to treat other types of infections caused by certain microorganisms such as fungi and protozoa. However, overuse and misuse of antibiotics has led to the emergence of resistant strains of bacteria. This phenomenon is known as "antibiotic resistance" (ABR).

An aortic aneurysm is a permanent dilation of the aorta that exceeds 50% of its normal diameter . It can occur in any part of the aorta but most commonly affects the abdominal aorta below the chest area or the thoracic aorta above the heart. If left untreated, it may rupture, leading to life-threatening bleeding. The exact cause of aortic aneurysms is unknown; however, certain factors such as smoking, family history, high blood pressure, and genetic conditions like Marfan syndrome increase the risk of developing them. Treatment options include medication management, lifestyle modifications, and surgery depending on the location and size of the aneurysm.
answers_noisy: The term you are referring to is an abscess. An abscess is defined as a collection of pus in a confined space within the body. It can occur anywhere in the body including the skin, lungs, brain, or internal organs such as the appendix. Abscesses typically form due to bacterial infection and may be caused by various factors such as trauma, foreign bodies, inflammatory conditions, or surgery. They often present as a localized area of redness, warmth, tenderness, and pain, which may be accompanied by fever and other systemic symptoms depending on their location. Diagnosis involves physical examination and imaging tests such as ultrasound, CT scan, or MRI. Treatment usually consists of antibiotics and surgical drainage if necessary.

Acute leukemias (AL) are malignant disorders of hematopoietic stem cells that result in an overproduction of immature white blood cells known as blast cells . These abnormal blast cells accumulate within the bone marrow and interfere with normal hematopoiesis, leading to pancytopenia or the production of abnormally differentiated peripheral blood cells . AL can be divided into two main types: myeloblastic leukemia (AML), which affects myeloid precursor cells, and lymphocytic leukemia (ALL), which affects lymphoid progenitor cells . The diagnosis of AL requires a combination of clinical symptoms, physical examination findings, laboratory tests such as complete blood count and bone marrow biopsy, and cytogenetic analysis . Treatment typically involves chemotherapy, often combined with targeted therapies depending on the specific subtype of AL .

The type of cancer you are referring to is called renal cell carcinoma (RCC). Renal cell carcinomas can occur anywhere within the kidney but most commonly arise from the proximal tubules . RCCs account for about 3% of all adult malignancies worldwide , with an estimated incidence rate of approximately one per 100,000 people per year .

Yes, I am familiar with poor air quality and its effects on health. Poor air quality is associated with an increased risk of respiratory diseases such as asthma, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), lung cancer, heart disease, stroke, diabetes, and other adverse health outcomes .

People who provide support to individuals with cognitive impairment may be referred to as caregivers or informal caregivers. They can include family members such as spouses, adult children, or other relatives; friends; neighbors; or unpaid professionals such as respite care providers.

Yes, I can provide information about exercise and physical activity for building or maintaining muscle mass. Regular resistance training is recommended to build and maintain muscle mass in adults over 30 years of age . Resistance training should be performed at least two days per week with exercises that work all major muscle groups . It is also important to consume adequate amounts of protein (at least 1 gram per pound of body weight) to support muscle growth during resistance training .

Anemia refers to a condition in which there is a decrease in the number of red blood cells (RBCs) or their ability to carry oxygen throughout the body. This can result in reduced levels of hemoglobin (Hb), a protein found within RBCs that carries oxygen from the lungs to other tissues.

If something bites you, such as an insect bite or dog bite, it can cause swelling, redness, pain, warmth, and sometimes drainage at the site of the injury. These symptoms may be mild to severe depending on the type of bite and whether there was infection present. Insect bites typically heal within a few days without any complications. However, in rare cases, they can lead to serious conditions such as Lyme disease or West Nile virus. Dog bites can also result in more serious consequences including rabies, tetanus, bacterial infections, nerve damage, disfigurement, and even death .

Antibiotic resistance is a growing concern in modern healthcare. It occurs when bacteria or other microorganisms develop mechanisms to evade the effects of antibiotics, rendering them less effective or completely ineffective. This can happen through several different mechanisms, including mutations within bacterial populations, horizontal gene transfer between resistant and susceptible strains, and de novo acquisition of resistance genes from environmental sources .

An aneurysm is a bulge or balloon-like enlargement of the blood vessel wall due to weakening of the vessel wall. It can occur anywhere in the body but most commonly occurs in large arteries such as the abdominal aorta, cerebral arteries, and coronary arteries. Aneurysms may be asymptomatic or cause symptoms depending on their location and size. If left untreated, they can rupture leading to life-threatening bleeding.
answers_repaired: A painful swollen lump containing pus is commonly known as an abscess. Abscesses can be caused by various factors such as bacterial infections (e.g., skin infections), parasites (e.g., cysticercosis), fungal infections (e.g., mycetoma), or other causes including foreign bodies, autoimmune diseases, trauma, neoplasms, and congenital anomalies.

Acute leukemia refers to a group of hematological malignancies characterized by uncontrolled proliferation of immature myeloid or lymphocytic precursors in the bone marrow (BM), blood, or other organs . These cells fail to differentiate into mature functional blood cells, resulting in pancytopenia with an accumulation of abnormal blast-like cells that can rapidly spread throughout the body via the circulatory system . The two main types of acute leukemias are acute myeloid leukemia (AML) and acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL). AML affects myeloid lineage cells while ALL affects lymphoid lineage cells.

Adrenocortical carcinoma (ACC) is a rare malignant tumor that arises from the cells of the outer layer of the adrenal cortex, which sits atop each kidney. It accounts for less than 2% of all cancers originating from endocrine tissue . The annual incidence rate of ACC ranges between 0.75 and 1.8 cases per million people worldwide .

Poor air quality or exposure to air pollutants has been associated with several adverse health outcomes including respiratory diseases such as asthma, bronchitis, pneumonia, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), lung cancer, cardiovascular diseases like heart attack, stroke, coronary artery disease, arrhythmia, hypertension, diabetes mellitus, reproductive disorders, premature delivery in pregnant women, low birth weight, preterm births, developmental defects in infants, cognitive impairment, mental retardation, reduced work capacity, increased absenteeism from schools and offices, decreased productivity at work, fatigue, headaches, eye irritations, dizziness, nausea, depression, anxiety, and sleep disturbances .

The healthcare professional who cares for people with significant memory problems is commonly referred to as a dementia care specialist or geriatrician. Their role involves assessing the patient's cognitive function, diagnosing dementia, providing treatment plans, and managing symptoms such as behavioral changes. They may also refer patients to other specialists, such as neurologists or psychiatrists, depending on their specific needs.

Muscle building supplements can cause adverse reactions such as nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, stomach pain, headache, dizziness, insomnia, irritability, dry mouth, increased sweating, and changes in menstrual cycle. Long term use may lead to kidney damage, liver problems, high blood pressure, heart disease, stroke, and even death (1).

Low blood volume or insufficient blood in the body is called hypovolemia. It occurs due to excessive bleeding, dehydration from vomiting and diarrhea, or fluid loss through burns or trauma. Symptoms include lightheadedness, dizziness, fainting (syncope), tachycardia, and decreased urine output.

Animal bites can be very dangerous depending on the type of animal involved in the attack. Bites from mammals such as dogs, cats, bears, and primates have the highest risk for infection due to their saliva containing bacteria that can cause severe illnesses like rabies, tetanus, and anthrax. Insect bites can also lead to serious health problems if the insects carry diseases such as malaria, dengue fever, West Nile virus, Lyme disease, etc. If you suspect any kind of animal or insect bite, especially if it involves the head or neck area, seek immediate medical attention. Do not attempt self-treatment with over-the-counter medications because this could worsen the condition.

Antibiotic resistance occurs when bacteria or other microorganisms become resistant to an antibiotic that was previously effective against them. This can occur through several mechanisms, including mutations in bacterial DNA, acquisition of new genetic material from other resistant strains, or horizontal gene transfer. These changes allow bacteria to produce enzymes that break down the drug, alter their cell walls so the drug cannot enter the cells, or pump out the drugs before they can do damage to the bacteria. The more we use antibiotics, the greater the chance that resistance will develop.

An aneurysm refers to a localized dilation or bulging of the blood vessel wall due to weakening of its structure. It can occur in any size blood vessels but more commonly affects medium-sized and large arteries such as the abdominal aorta, cerebral arteries, coronary arteries, popliteal arteries, and pulmonary arteries. Aneurysms may be asymptomatic or present with symptoms depending on their location. If ruptured, they can cause life-threatening bleeding that requires immediate surgical intervention.
reference_answers: An abscess is a pocket of pus. You can get an abscess almost anywhere in your body. When an area of your body becomes infected, your body's immune system tries to fight the infection. White blood cells go to the infected area, collect within the damaged tissue, and cause inflammation. During this process, pus forms. Pus is a mixture of living and dead white blood cells, germs, and dead tissue. Bacteria, viruses, parasites and swallowed objects can all lead to abscesses. Skin abscesses are easy to detect. They are red, raised and painful. Abscesses inside your body may not be obvious and can damage organs, including the brain, lungs and others. Treatments include drainage and antibiotics.

Leukemia is cancer of the white blood cells. White blood cells help your body fight infection. Your blood cells form in your bone marrow. In leukemia, however, the bone marrow produces abnormal white blood cells. These cells crowd out the healthy blood cells, making it hard for blood to do its work. In acute lymphocytic leukemia (ALL), also called acute lymphoblastic leukemia, there are too many of specific types of white blood cells called lymphocytes or lymphoblasts. ALL is the most common type of cancer in children. Possible risk factors for ALL include being male, being white, previous chemotherapy treatment, exposure to radiation, and for adults, being older than 70. Symptoms of ALL include: - Weakness or feeling tired - Fever - Easy bruising or bleeding - Bleeding under the skin - Shortness of breath - Weight loss or loss of appetite - Pain in the bones or stomach - Pain or a feeling of fullness below the ribs - Painless lumps in the neck, underarm, stomach, or groin Tests that examine the blood and bone marrow diagnose ALL. Treatments include chemotherapy, radiation therapy, stem cell transplants, and targeted therapy. Targeted therapy uses substances that attack cancer cells without harming normal cells. Once the leukemia is in remission, you need additional treatment to make sure that it does not come back. NIH: National Cancer Institute

Your adrenal, or suprarenal, glands are located on the top of each kidney. These glands produce hormones that you can't live without, including sex hormones and cortisol, which helps you respond to stress and has many other functions. A number of disorders can affect the adrenal glands, including tumors. Tumors can be either benign or malignant. Benign tumors aren't cancer. Malignant ones are. Most adrenal gland tumors are benign. They usually do not cause symptoms and may not require treatment. Malignant adrenal gland cancers are uncommon. Types of tumors include - Adrenocortical carcinoma - cancer in the outer part of the gland - Neuroblastoma, a type of childhood cancer - Pheochromocytoma - a rare tumor that is usually benign Symptoms depend on the type of cancer you have. Treatments may include surgery, chemotherapy, or radiation therapy.

Summary : Air pollution is a mixture of solid particles and gases in the air. Car emissions, chemicals from factories, dust, pollen and mold spores may be suspended as particles. Ozone, a gas, is a major part of air pollution in cities. When ozone forms air pollution, it's also called smog. Some air pollutants are poisonous. Inhaling them can increase the chance you'll have health problems. People with heart or lung disease, older adults and children are at greater risk from air pollution. Air pollution isn't just outside - the air inside buildings can also be polluted and affect your health. Environmental Protection Agency

Caring for someone who has Alzheimer's disease (AD) can be stressful and overwhelming. It's important to take care of yourself. Ask for and accept help. Talk to the doctor. Find out what treatments might help control symptoms or address behavior problems. Find a support group. Others who have "been there" may be able to help and will understand. If there are times of day that the person is less confused or more cooperative, take advantage of that in daily routines. Consider using adult day care or respite services. These offer a break with the peace of mind that the patient is being taken care of. Begin to plan for the future. This may include - Getting financial and legal documents in order - Looking into assisted living or nursing homes - Finding out what your health insurance and Medicare will cover NIH: National Institute on Aging

Summary : Anabolic steroids are man-made substances related to male sex hormones. Doctors use anabolic steroids to treat some hormone problems in men, delayed puberty, and muscle loss from some diseases. Bodybuilders and athletes often use anabolic steroids to build muscles and improve athletic performance. Using them this way is not legal or safe. Abuse of anabolic steroids has been linked with many health problems. They include - Acne - Breast growth and shrinking of testicles in men - Voice deepening and growth of facial hair in women - High blood pressure - Heart problems, including heart attack - Liver disease, including cancer - Kidney damage - Aggressive behavior NIH: National Institute on Drug Abuse

If you have anemia, your blood does not carry enough oxygen to the rest of your body. The most common cause of anemia is not having enough iron. Your body needs iron to make hemoglobin. Hemoglobin is an iron-rich protein that gives the red color to blood. It carries oxygen from the lungs to the rest of the body. Anemia has three main causes: blood loss, lack of red blood cell production, and high rates of red blood cell destruction. Conditions that may lead to anemia include - Heavy periods - Pregnancy - Ulcers - Colon polyps or colon cancer - Inherited disorders - A diet that does not have enough iron, folic acid or vitamin B12 - Blood disorders such as sickle cell anemia and thalassemia, or cancer - Aplastic anemia, a condition that can be inherited or acquired - G6PD deficiency, a metabolic disorder Anemia can make you feel tired, cold, dizzy, and irritable. You may be short of breath or have a headache. Your doctor will diagnose anemia with a physical exam and blood tests. Treatment depends on the kind of anemia you have. NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute

Wild animals usually avoid people. They might attack, however, if they feel threatened, are sick, or are protecting their young or territory. Attacks by pets are more common. Animal bites rarely are life-threatening, but if they become infected, you can develop serious medical problems. To prevent animal bites and complications from bites - Never pet, handle, or feed unknown animals - Leave snakes alone - Watch your children closely around animals - Vaccinate your cats, ferrets, and dogs against rabies - Spay or neuter your dog to make it less aggressive - Get a tetanus booster if you have not had one recently - Wear boots and long pants when you are in areas with venomous snakes If an animal bites you, clean the wound with soap and water as soon as possible. Get medical attention if necessary. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention

Summary : Antibiotics are medicines that fight bacterial infections. Used properly, they can save lives. But there is a growing problem of antibiotic resistance. It happens when bacteria change and become able to resist the effects of an antibiotic. Using antibiotics can lead to resistance. Each time you take antibiotics, sensitive bacteria are killed. But resistant germs may be left to grow and multiply. They can spread to other people. They can also cause infections that certain antibiotics cannot cure. Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) is one example. It causes infections that are resistant to several common antibiotics. To help prevent antibiotic resistance - Don't use antibiotics for viruses like colds or flu. Antibiotics don't work on viruses. - Don't pressure your doctor to give you an antibiotic. - When you take antibiotics, follow the directions carefully. Finish your medicine even if you feel better. If you stop treatment too soon, some bacteria may survive and re-infect you. - Don't save antibiotics for later or use someone else's prescription. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention

An aneurysm is a bulge or "ballooning" in the wall of an artery. Arteries are blood vessels that carry oxygen-rich blood from the heart to other parts of the body. If an aneurysm grows large, it can burst and cause dangerous bleeding or even death. Most aneurysms are in the aorta, the main artery that runs from the heart through the chest and abdomen. There are two types of aortic aneurysm: - Thoracic aortic aneurysms - these occur in the part of the aorta running through the chest - Abdominal aortic aneurysms - these occur in the part of the aorta running through the abdomen Most aneurysms are found during tests done for other reasons. Some people are at high risk for aneurysms. It is important for them to get screening, because aneurysms can develop and become large before causing any symptoms Screening is recommended for people between the ages of 65 and 75 if they have a family history, or if they are men who have smoked. Doctors use imaging tests to find aneurysms. Medicines and surgery are the two main treatments. NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute

answers_clean: An abortion is the intentional termination of a pregnancy before it can be carried to full-term development . This procedure can be performed either through medication or surgery depending on how far along the pregnancy is. Medication abortions typically use a combination of mifepristone and misoprostol to induce an abortion up until 63 days after conception. Surgical abortions include dilation and evacuation procedures such as manual vacuum aspiration (MVA), curettage, hysterectomy, or salpingostomy .

Acute bronchitis refers to an inflammation of the airways in the lungs caused by infection with viruses or bacteria . It typically presents as coughing that lasts for less than three weeks, along with other symptoms such as sore throat, runny nose, sneezing, fever, chills, chest congestion, wheezing, shortness of breath, fatigue, and sometimes nausea and vomiting . In most cases, acute bronchitis resolves without treatment within two weeks; however, antibiotics may be prescribed if there is concern about secondary bacterial infection .

Yes, I do have some information about adoption. Adoption is the legal process by which a child who has lost one or both parents becomes part of another family. It involves termination of parental rights over the child and transferring these to adoptive parents. The main goal of adoption is to provide a permanent home for children in need and to ensure that they grow up with loving families.

There can be several complications after surgery, including infection, bleeding, pain, swelling, scarring, and adhesions. It is important to follow your doctor's instructions for post-operative care to minimize these risks.

Alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency (AATD), also known as AAT deficiency or PiZ deficiency, is an inherited disorder that affects the production of alpha-1 antitrypsin (AAT), a serine protease inhibitor produced by hepatocytes in the liver . The primary function of AAT is to regulate inflammation by preventing excessive activation of neutrophil elastases during acute inflammatory responses .

Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), also known as Lou Gehrig's disease, is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder that affects motor neurons in both the brain and spinal cord . It causes muscle weakness, atrophy, and paralysis, which can lead to difficulty speaking, swallowing, and walking, and eventually death due to respiratory failure . The exact cause of ALS remains unknown, but it has been linked to genetic factors, environmental exposures, and other risk factors such as smoking or head injury . Diagnosis involves a combination of clinical evaluation, neurological examination, electrodiagnostic testing, and imaging studies . There is no cure for ALS, but treatments can help manage symptoms and improve quality of life .

Yes, I do! Angioplasty is also known as percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI). It involves using a catheter to access the narrowed or blocked artery in the heart through the blood vessels of the wrist or groin. A balloon at the tip of the catheter is then inflated to compress the plaque against the wall of the artery, thus widening it. In some cases, a stent may be placed into the artery to help keep it open. This procedure can improve blood flow to the heart and reduce chest pain.

Anthrax is an infectious disease caused by the bacterium Bacillus anthracis. It can occur in three forms: cutaneous, gastrointestinal, or inhalational. Cutaneous anthrax is characterized by one or more skin lesions that begin as small, painless sores that develop into large, ulcerative lesions with black centers. Gastrointestinal anthrax causes symptoms such as fever, abdominal pain, vomiting, and diarrhea. Inhalation anthrax affects the lungs and may be fatal if not treated promptly.

Anxiety is an emotion characterized by feelings of tension, worry, or unease. It can be accompanied by physical symptoms such as increased heart rate, sweating, and muscle tension. While some anxiety is normal in response to stressful situations, excessive or persistent anxiety may indicate an underlying mental health condition such as generalized anxiety disorder (GAD). GAD is a chronic and debilitating illness that causes individuals to experience disproportionate levels of fear and anxiety about everyday events for no apparent reason. The symptoms often interfere with daily activities and quality of life.
answers_noisy: I apologize for any confusion in my previous response. It appears there may have been some misunderstanding regarding the terminology used to describe pregnancy outcomes. The term "ending" can be interpreted as referring to either an induced abortion (also known as termination of pregnancy) or spontaneous fetal loss prior to 20 weeks gestation (miscarriage).

The condition you are referring to is known as post-nasal drip syndrome (PNDS). PNDS can be caused by various factors such as allergies, sinusitis, infection, or reflux from the stomach. It may also occur due to changes in weather conditions or seasonality. Symptoms include excessive nasal discharge, sneezing, itchy eyes, sore throat, hoarseness, and cough with phlegm production. Treatment options depend on the underlying cause.

Yes, I can provide some information about this topic. When a child is removed from their biological parents due to abuse, neglect, or other safety concerns, they may be placed into foster care with a licensed foster family home. The decision to remove a child from their parents is made by Child Protective Services (CPS) after an investigation of reported maltreatment. If CPS determines that there is risk to the child's health or safety remaining in the home, they will work with the court system to determine the best course of action for ensuring the child's well-being. This may include reuniting the child with their parents if it is safe to do so, placing them in foster care, or terminating parental rights and initiating permanent placement proceedings such as adoption.

After receiving treatment in the hospital for an illness or injury, there is always a possibility of complications that may arise as a result of the procedure or medication administered during your stay. These complications could include allergic reactions to medications, bleeding, infection, blood clots, heart attacks, strokes, and even death. It's important to follow up with your doctor after being discharged from the hospital to monitor any potential complications and ensure proper recovery.

The condition you may be referring to is called alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency (AATD). It is an inherited disorder caused by mutations in the SERPINA1 gene, which encodes for the AAT protein. This protein plays a crucial role in protecting the lungs from damage due to inflammation or infection.

The condition you are referring to is likely amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), also known as Lou Gehrig's Disease. It is not related to other diseases causing muscle weakness such as polymyositis or myasthenia gravis. ALS causes progressive degeneration of motor neurons in both the brain and spinal cord, leading to muscle atrophy and weakness throughout the body. This can result in difficulty speaking, swallowing, walking, and performing other daily activities. While there is no cure for ALS, treatments can help manage symptoms and improve quality of life.

The human body consists of several systems that work together to maintain health and perform various functions necessary for life. These include the circulatory system (heart, blood vessels, and blood), digestive system (mouth, esophagus, stomach, small intestine, large intestine, liver, gallbladder, and pancreas), respiratory system (nose, pharynx, larynx, trachea, bronchi, lungs, and pleura), urinary system (kidneys, ureters, bladder, and urethra), endocrine system (hypothalamus, pituitary gland, thyroid gland, parathyroid glands, adrenal glands, ovaries or testes, pancreas, and skin), nervous system (brain, spinal cord, nerves, ganglia), muscular system (skeletal muscles), integumentary system (skin), skeletal system (bones, cartilage, ligaments, tendons, and joints). Each organ has specific functions that contribute to maintaining homeostasis in the body. For example, the heart pumps blood throughout the body via the circulatory system; the kidneys filter waste products from the blood; the lungs take in oxygen and release carbon dioxide; and the brain controls bodily functions through signals sent by neurons.

Yes, I can provide information about this procedure known as coronary angioplasty with stent placement. Coronary artery disease (CAD) is one of the leading causes of death worldwide . CAD occurs when the coronary arteries become narrowed due to plaque buildup, which reduces the amount of oxygen-rich blood flowing to the heart muscle. This can lead to chest pain, shortness of breath, and other symptoms.

The condition you may be referring to is called monkeypox. Monkeypox is caused by the monkeypox virus (MPXV), which belongs to the same family of viruses as smallpox but causes milder symptoms . It was first discovered in 1958 among laboratory-bred macaque monkeys at the Institute for Medical Research in Côte d’Ivoire, Africa .

The condition you described may be referred to as anxiety or panic attacks. Anxiety can be caused by stressful events in one's life, but sometimes occurs without an apparent trigger. Panic attacks are sudden episodes of intense fear or discomfort that peak within minutes and include several physical symptoms such as rapid heartbeat, sweating, trembling, and difficulty breathing. It is important to consult with your healthcare provider if you experience these symptoms frequently or severely enough to interfere with daily activities. They will likely ask about any recent stressors in your life and assess whether further testing or treatment is necessary.
answers_repaired: An abortion refers to the termination of an unwanted or ectopic pregnancy before 20 weeks of gestation. It can be performed by various methods such as surgical (dilation and curettage) or medical abortion using medications like mifepristone and misoprostol. The procedure involves removing fetal tissue from the uterus through dilation and suctioning or scraping with a curette. Medical abortions involve taking pills at home followed by a second dose after 48 hours if needed. This causes the lining of the uterus to break down and detach from the embryo, leading to its expulsion.

The medical term for this condition is tracheitis. It can be caused by an inflammation in the airways due to viral or bacterial infections, which leads to swelling and narrowing of the trachea. This can cause difficulty in breathing, coughing, and production of excess secretions.

In foster care, children who have been removed from their biological families due to abuse, neglect, or other reasons beyond parental control are placed with licensed foster parents for temporary placement . The goal of foster care is to provide a safe environment while maintaining contact between the child and his or her biological family whenever possible. If reunification with the biological family is not feasible after 15 months in foster care, the child may become eligible for adoption, which can be initiated by either the birth parents or the legal guardian(s) . Adoption involves permanent transfer of all rights and responsibilities from the biological parents to adoptive parents, who assume responsibility for raising the child until adulthood.

These may include bleeding, infection at the site of surgery, allergic reactions to medications, changes in mental status such as confusion or lethargy, chest pain or shortness of breath, abdominal pain or swelling, fever, nausea or vomiting, diarrhea, constipation, fatigue, dizziness, fainting spells, difficulty urinating, numbness or tingling sensations, severe headaches, seizures, loss of vision, and skin rashes . If any of these complications or symptoms occur, it is important to seek medical help immediately.

It is an autosomal recessive disorder caused by mutations in the SERPINA1 gene, resulting in insufficient production or function of the serine protease inhibitor alpha-1 antitrypsin (α1AT), a key regulator of inflammation. AATD affects approximately 1 in every 2000 people worldwide .

It includes the mouth, esophagus, stomach, small intestine, large intestine (colon), liver, gallbladder, and pancreas. Angioplasty involves inserting a thin tube (catheter) with a balloon at its tip through a small incision in the groin or arm into the narrowed or blocked coronary artery. The catheter is then threaded up towards the heart. Once it reaches the blockage, the balloon is inflated to widen the artery and restore blood flow. Afterwards, the catheter may be removed, or a stent can be placed inside the artery to keep it open permanently.

Anthrax is an infectious disease caused by Bacillus anthracis bacteria that typically affects herbivorous animals but can also affect humans. There are three forms of human infection: cutaneous (skin), gastrointestinal (GI) and inhalational. Inhalation anthrax occurs when spores enter through the nose or mouth into the lungs where they germinate and cause pneumonia. This form of anthrax is highly lethal if not treated promptly.

This condition is known as a “panic attack.” It can be indicative of an anxiety or panic disorder but may also occur due to other factors such as stressful situations, caffeine consumption, or physical exertion. Panic attacks typically last only minutes rather than hours and often peak within 10 min.
reference_answers: An abortion is a procedure to end a pregnancy. It uses medicine or surgery to remove the embryo or fetus and placenta from the uterus. The procedure is done by a licensed health care professional. The decision to end a pregnancy is very personal. If you are thinking of having an abortion, most healthcare providers advise counseling.

Bronchitis is an inflammation of the bronchial tubes, the airways that carry air to your lungs. It causes a cough that often brings up mucus. It can also cause shortness of breath, wheezing, a low fever, and chest tightness. There are two main types of bronchitis: acute and chronic. Most cases of acute bronchitis get better within several days. But your cough can last for several weeks after the infection is gone. The same viruses that cause colds and the flu often cause acute bronchitis. These viruses spread through the air when people cough, or though physical contact (for example, on unwashed hands). Being exposed to tobacco smoke, air pollution, dusts, vapors, and fumes can also cause acute bronchitis. Less often, bacteria can also cause acute bronchitis. To diagnose acute bronchitis, your health care provider will ask about your symptoms and listen to your breathing. You may also have other tests. Treatments include rest, fluids, and aspirin (for adults) or acetaminophen to treat fever. A humidifier or steam can also help. You may need inhaled medicine to open your airways if you are wheezing. Antibiotics won't help if the cause is viral. You may get antibiotics if the cause is bacterial. NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute

Summary : Adoption brings a child born to other parents into a new family. Birth parents have a number of reasons for placing children for adoption. Overall, they want better lives for their children than they think they can give them. Children who are eligible for adoption come from many different settings. Some are in foster care, a temporary home setting. Other children live in orphanages or with birth relatives until they can be adopted. There are different kinds of adoption. Children may be adopted by a relative or a new family. Some parents adopt children from the U.S, and some adopt from abroad.

After any operation, you'll have some side effects. There is usually some pain with surgery. There may also be swelling and soreness around the area that the surgeon cut. Your surgeon can tell you which side effects to expect. There can also be complications. These are unplanned events linked to the operation. Some complications are infection, too much bleeding, reaction to anesthesia, or accidental injury. Some people have a greater risk of complications because of other medical conditions. Your surgeon can tell you how you might feel and what you will be able to do - or not do - the first few days, weeks, or months after surgery. Some other questions to ask are - How long you will be in the hospital - What kind of supplies, equipment, and help you might need when you go home - When you can go back to work - When it is ok to start exercising again - Are they any other restrictions in your activities Following your surgeon's advice can help you recover as soon as possible. Agency for Healthcare Quality and Research

Alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency (AAT deficiency) is an inherited condition that raises your risk for lung and liver disease. Alpha-1 antitrypsin (AAT) is a protein that protects the lungs. The liver makes it. If the AAT proteins aren't the right shape, they get stuck in the liver cells and can't reach the lungs. Symptoms of AAT deficiency include - Shortness of breath and wheezing - Repeated lung infections - Tiredness - Rapid heartbeat upon standing - Vision problems - Weight loss Some people have no symptoms and do not develop complications. Blood tests and genetic tests can tell if you have it. If your lungs are affected, you may also have lung tests. Treatments include medicines, pulmonary rehab, and extra oxygen, if needed. Severe cases may need a lung transplant. Not smoking can prevent or delay lung symptoms. NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute

Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) is a nervous system disease that attacks nerve cells called neurons in your brain and spinal cord. These neurons transmit messages from your brain and spinal cord to your voluntary muscles - the ones you can control, like in your arms and legs. At first, this causes mild muscle problems. Some people notice - Trouble walking or running - Trouble writing - Speech problems Eventually, you lose your strength and cannot move. When muscles in your chest fail, you cannot breathe. A breathing machine can help, but most people with ALS die from respiratory failure. The disease usually strikes between age 40 and 60. More men than women get it. No one knows what causes ALS. It can run in families, but usually it strikes at random. There is no cure. Medicines can relieve symptoms and, sometimes, prolong survival. NIH: National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke
Summary : Anatomy is the science that studies the structure of the body. On this page, you'll find links to descriptions and pictures of the human body's parts and organ systems from head to toe.

Summary : If you have coronary artery disease, the arteries in your heart are narrowed or blocked by a sticky material called plaque. Angioplasty is a procedure to restore blood flow through the artery. You have angioplasty in a hospital. The doctor threads a thin tube through a blood vessel in the arm or groin up to the involved site in the artery. The tube has a tiny balloon on the end. When the tube is in place, the doctor inflates the balloon to push the plaque outward against the wall of the artery. This widens the artery and restores blood flow. Doctors may use angioplasty to - Reduce chest pain caused by reduced blood flow to the heart - Minimize damage to heart muscle from a heart attack Many people go home the day after angioplasty, and are able to return to work within a week of coming home. NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute

Anthrax is a disease caused by Bacillus anthracis, a germ that lives in soil. Many people know about it from the 2001 bioterror attacks. In the attacks, someone purposely spread anthrax through the U.S. mail. This killed five people and made 22 sick. Anthrax is rare. It affects animals such as cattle, sheep, and goats more often than people. People can get anthrax from contact with infected animals, wool, meat, or hides. It can cause three forms of disease in people. They are - Cutaneous, which affects the skin. People with cuts or open sores can get it if they touch the bacteria. - Inhalation, which affects the lungs. You can get this if you breathe in spores of the bacteria. - Gastrointestinal, which affects the digestive system. You can get it by eating infected meat. Antibiotics often cure anthrax if it is diagnosed early. But many people don't know they have anthrax until it is too late to treat. A vaccine to prevent anthrax is available for people in the military and others at high risk. NIH: National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases

Fear and anxiety are part of life. You may feel anxious before you take a test or walk down a dark street. This kind of anxiety is useful - it can make you more alert or careful. It usually ends soon after you are out of the situation that caused it. But for millions of people in the United States, the anxiety does not go away, and gets worse over time. They may have chest pains or nightmares. They may even be afraid to leave home. These people have anxiety disorders. Types include - Panic disorder - Obsessive-compulsive disorder - Post-traumatic stress disorder - Phobias - Generalized anxiety disorder Treatment can involve medicines, therapy or both. NIH: National Institute of Mental Health
